blurb: The well-behaved native goldenrod: dense club-shaped plumes of gold light up the fall garden on tidy upright clumps — a keystone late-season nectar source for bees and migrating monarchs, and (despite the myth) not the cause of hay fever.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
spread: Clumping and well-behaved — a non-running goldenrod that stays put, unlike the weedy Canada goldenrod
seasons: Upright leafy clumps through summer; showy golden pyramidal plumes in late summer and fall; tan seedheads stand into winter
wildlife: A keystone late-season nectar plant — bees, wasps, butterflies and migrating monarchs; seeds feed birds; larval host for many moths
toxic: Non-toxic — and NOT the cause of hay fever (that's wind-pollinated ragweed, which blooms at the same time)

care.hardiness: A tough, long-lived Great Plains and eastern-prairie native whose range runs well north of Colorado, so it is fully hardy through Boulder winters (USDA zone 5b–6a) with no protection.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out container plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall while the soil is still workable; water in to establish, then taper off. It is drought- and clay-tolerant once rooted. In a pot it needs a gritty, free-draining mix and is best sunk in the ground or moved to a sheltered spot over winter, since confined roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground plants.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun. Our intense high-altitude light is ideal; in shade the stems stretch, lean, and flower poorly.[1][3]
care.soil: Lean, well-drained soil — it tolerates Boulder's alkaline clay well and needs no pH amendment.[3] Keep it on the lean side: rich, fertile, or over-watered ground makes the clumps grow tall and floppy and flop open.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established. Water occasionally through the first season to root it in, then little supplemental water — it is drought-tolerant and resents wet feet.[1][3]
care.spacing: Space plants about 18–24 inches apart; the clumps stay tidy and do not run, so they hold their spot in a border.[1]
care.propagation: Increase by seed or by division. Unlike the aggressively running Canada goldenrod, showy goldenrod forms tight, well-behaved clumps that divide cleanly in early spring or fall every few years to rejuvenate and make more plants.[1][2]
care.sow: Surface-sow the tiny seed onto firm soil and press in but do not bury it — the seed needs light to germinate. Sow outdoors in late fall to let winter provide the cold-moist period, or start cold-stratified seed indoors in spring.[1][2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed about 30–60 days at ~40°F (or sow in fall for natural winter stratification) to break dormancy.[1][2]
care.bloom: Blooms late summer into fall — roughly September into October on the Front Range — in dense, club-shaped golden plumes, one of the latest and most valuable nectar sources of the season.[1][3]
care.feeding: None needed. Fertile soil or feeding produces lush, weak, floppy growth; lean ground keeps the clump upright and self-supporting.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low. Leave the seedheads standing for winter structure and birds, then cut the old stems to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges. Cutting clumps back by a third in early summer makes a shorter, sturdier, bushier plant if floppiness is a worry.[1]
care.selfsow: It will self-sow modestly from seed but does not run at the root, so seedlings are easy to pull or move and it never colonizes the way Canada goldenrod does.[1][2]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free; goldenrods are not the cause of hay fever (their pollen is heavy and insect-carried — the real culprit is wind-pollinated ragweed blooming at the same time). Floppiness from rich soil or too much water is the main complaint; rust or mildew is occasional on crowded plants.[1][3]
care.companions: Pair with little bluestem and other native bunchgrasses, asters, blanketflower, and Rocky Mountain bee plant in a full-sun, lean-soil, low-water planting for a strong fall pollinator display.[3]

edible.summary: Showy goldenrod is grown as an ornamental and keystone pollinator plant; the documented Native American use of this species is medicinal, not culinary, so it is not treated as a food here.[4]
edible.caution: The authoritative ethnobotanical record for Solidago speciosa lists only medicinal (root infusion/decoction) uses, with no documented food or beverage use, so don't eat it as food.[4] Goldenrods as a genus are sometimes brewed as an herbal tea, but that is not specifically documented for this species and is best left to those who know the plant — be certain of identification first. One thing it is NOT: a cause of hay fever. Goldenrod pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not airborne; the sneezing blamed on it comes from wind-pollinated ragweed blooming at the same time.[1][4]
